analysis.bottleneck_summary: 19 of 21 operators classified tensor_core_idle. Every GEMM dispatches to ampere_sgemm_128x64_tn (FP32 SIMT, 0% Tensor Core utilization). The logit projection (512->32000) alone accounts for 37.7% of attributed time. Total GEMM operators account for ~99.7% of attributed time. The embedding+LayerNorm kernel is already fused by Inductor and well-optimized.

bottleneck.description: All GEMMs run as FP32 SIMT via ampere_sgemm_128x64_tn with 0% Tensor Core utilization. On Ampere, the BF16 Tensor Core (HMMA) path delivers up to 312 TFLOPS vs. ~19.5 TFLOPS for FP32 SIMT — a 16x theoretical peak difference. The model runs entirely in FP32 throughout with no dtype promotion applied. The logit projection is SM-saturating (sm_throughput_pct ~97%) but wasting nearly all SM cycles on scalar FP32 MADs rather than HMMA instructions.

transformation.description: Cast model and input to torch.bfloat16. On Ampere this routes all matrix multiplications from ampere_sgemm_128x64_tn (SIMT, scalar FP32) to sm80_xmma_gemm_bf16bf16 (HMMA Tensor Core). The embedding table and logit weight are separate module instances (not tied), so the cast is safe. Token IDs are integer tensors and are unaffected.
fx_steps: model = model.to(torch.bfloat16)
# token_ids are integer indices — do NOT cast; embedding will output BF16 automatically
transformation.code_hint: Apply BEFORE torch.compile(). Dynamo traces dtype as a static property; if the model is cast inside the compiled region the graph will contain explicit to() nodes that inhibit kernel selection. Cast before compilation so the compiled graph is monomorphic BF16 throughout.
transformation.safe_for_embedding: Yes — nn.Embedding stores float weights and produces float outputs matching the weight dtype. After model.to(bfloat16), embed.weight is BF16 and embedding outputs are BF16, feeding directly into the BF16 LayerNorm and subsequent GEMMs.
transformation.tied_weights_check: NOT tied: self.embed (nn.Embedding, weight shape [32000,512]) and self.logits (nn.Linear, weight shape [32000,512]) are distinct parameter tensors. No tied-embedding contraindication applies.

estimated_impact.reasoning: The logit projection alone is 37.7% of time and is SM-saturating (sm_throughput ~97%). After BF16 dtype promotion the kernel changes to HMMA. With waves_per_sm=297 the large GEMM is not wave-starved; arithmetic throughput is the direct ceiling. Expected 4-8x speedup on the logit projection is realistic (BF16 TC vs. FP32 SIMT at same SM count), yielding a 25-30% absolute time reduction on that op. Applying uniformly across all 99.7% GEMM time yields 50-80% total wall-time reduction.
estimated_impact.reference: A100 SXM4 spec: 19.5 TFLOPS FP32 SIMT vs. 312 TFLOPS BF16 Tensor Core (16x peak ratio). Realized ratio for large, compute-bound GEMMs typically 4-8x after accounting for memory bandwidth and pipeline fill.

operators: aten::mm op_ids 50,60,70,80,90,100 (logit projection [8192,512]x[512,32000], repeated per layer)

bottleneck.description: The logit projection weight (shape [32000, 512]) is called once per layer in the stack. Each invocation dispatches an independent GEMM. In the profile, op_ids 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 100 each account for ~8.85% of total time with waves_per_sm=297 — the GEMM is large enough that batching would not reduce wave count. However, all six invocations use the same weight matrix, and if the activations can be concatenated along the batch dimension (M axis), a single wider GEMM is a cleaner computation for the memory subsystem (amortizes weight loads) and allows cuBLAS to pick a larger tile configuration.

evidence.note: High L2 hit rate on weight already — weight is cached across layers. However, 6 separate kernel launches carry 6x launch overhead and prevent cuBLAS from selecting the widest blocking factor.

transformation.description: FX pass to batch consecutive logit projection calls that share the same weight into a single mm over a concatenated activation batch, then split the result. Applicable only if all activations are available before the first logit mm executes (i.e., they are not sequentially dependent on each other's logit output). Inspect the graph: if the activations feeding op_ids 50-100 are independent (each from a separate FFN stack operating on different tokens/layers without cross-dependency), stack them.
transformation.location: FX pass applied to the compiled graph, OR restructure the model forward() to call self.logits once on a stacked input
fx_steps: # Option A — Model-level rewrite (lower risk, same effect):
# In EmbeddingProjection.forward(), accumulate hidden states across layers,
# then call self.logits once on the stacked tensor:
#   hidden_list = [h1, h2, h3, h4, h5, h6]  # (B*T, DIM) each
#   hidden_stacked = torch.stack(hidden_list, dim=0).reshape(-1, DIM)  # (6*B*T, DIM)
#   logits_all = self.logits(hidden_stacked)  # single GEMM: (6*B*T, 32000)
#   logits_list = logits_all.reshape(6, B*T, VOCAB_SIZE).unbind(0)
# Option B — FX pass (if model source cannot be modified):
# 1. In the FX graph, identify all mm nodes where the second argument
#    (weight node) is the same get_attr node for logits.weight.
# 2. Verify no data dependency between them (they are in separate
#    sequential FFN blocks, not a recurrent loop).
# 3. Insert a cat node over all activation inputs before the first mm.
# 4. Replace all mm nodes with one mm node using the cat output.
# 5. Insert chunk/split nodes to restore individual outputs.
# 6. Replace all uses of the original mm outputs with the split outputs.
transformation.code_hint: This optimization is only valid if the 6 logit projections are NOT sequentially dependent (each layer's logit output does not feed the next layer's input). Inspect the input_op_ids: op_50 consumes (49,0),(39,0),(29,0) — these are hidden states from prior FFN layers, not prior logit outputs. Batching is safe.
transformation.caveat: If the model is used for autoregressive generation where only the last token's logits are needed, the intermediate logit projections may be dead computation. Consider removing them entirely before applying this batching optimization.

estimated_impact.reasoning: Replacing 6 kernel launches with 1 eliminates 5 kernel launch overheads (~5-10 us each) and allows cuBLAS to select a blocking factor tuned to 6x the M dimension. The weight is already L2-cached across launches (94.5% L2 hit), so DRAM savings are minimal. Primary gain is from cuBLAS tile efficiency improvement and launch serialization reduction. Conservative 6% total time estimate.
estimated_impact.prerequisite_satisfied: Must be applied after OPT-1 (BF16 cast) so the batched GEMM operates on BF16 tensors and routes to the Tensor Core path.

bottleneck.description: All mm nodes have a transposed weight: the pattern is mm(activation, W.T) where W is the stored parameter. This causes cuBLAS to execute a TN (transposed-normal) GEMM rather than NN. The kernel name ampere_sgemm_128x64_tn confirms the T suffix (transposed A operand). Pre-transposing the weight at initialization eliminates the on-the-fly transpose and switches cuBLAS to NN mode, which allows a wider set of blocking configurations on Ampere.

evidence.note: All weights exceed the 1 MB threshold for pre-transposition to be worthwhile.

transformation.description: FX pass to pre-transpose Linear weights at module initialization (or as a post-compile buffer registration), eliminating the aten.t() node before each mm. This switches the GEMM from TN to NN layout, allowing cuBLAS to select NN-optimized kernels (ampere_sgemm_*_nn variants). Must be applied after BF16 cast so the pre-transposed buffer is BF16.
transformation.location: FX pass on compiled graph, or pre-processing hook in get_model_and_input()
fx_steps: # Apply after model.to(torch.bfloat16) in get_model_and_input():
# For each Linear layer whose weight exceeds 512*512*2 bytes (1MB BF16):
for name, module in model.named_modules():
if isinstance(module, torch.nn.Linear):
W = module.weight  # shape: [out_features, in_features]
# weight is already stored as [out, in]; nn.Linear uses F.linear which
# internally calls mm(input, weight.T) — cuBLAS sees a TN GEMM.
# Pre-transposing: store as [in, out] contiguous so cuBLAS sees NN.
if W.numel() * W.element_size() >= 1024 * 1024:
with torch.no_grad():
module.weight = torch.nn.Parameter(
W.T.contiguous(),  # shape: [in_features, out_features]
requires_grad=False
# NOTE: After pre-transposing, F.linear(x, weight) calls mm(x, weight) directly
# (no .T), which cuBLAS dispatches as NN. This requires the forward pass to
# call mm(x, module.weight) instead of F.linear. If using F.linear, you must
# also monkey-patch the linear forward or replace with a custom module.
# Safer approach — replace F.linear with explicit mm in an FX pass:
# Find all call_function nodes for aten.linear / aten.addmm.
# For each: check if weight get_attr feeds through aten.t().
# Replace aten.t(weight) with a pre-computed buffer registered as
# gm.register_buffer('weight_T', weight.T.contiguous()).
# Replace the original mm/addmm with mm(x, weight_T_buf).
transformation.code_hint: The logit projection weight [32000, 512] pre-transposed becomes [512, 32000] — 33.6 MB in BF16. Memory cost doubles (keep both original for gradient / reload purposes, or replace in-place for inference-only). For inference only (eval mode), in-place replacement is safe.

estimated_impact.reasoning: On Ampere, NN vs. TN selection unlocks different blocking tile configurations. For very large GEMMs (waves_per_sm=297) the tile efficiency difference is small because cuBLAS already achieves near-peak utilization. For the smaller FFN ops (waves_per_sm=5-19) the impact is larger proportionally but those ops are <1% of total time each. Combined effect estimated at 2-8% total time reduction, applied after OPT-1.
estimated_impact.prerequisite_satisfied: Apply after OPT-1 so pre-transposed buffers are BF16, matching runtime dtype.

bottleneck.description: torch.compile with default mode does not run autotuning across cuBLAS algorithm choices. The logit projection (waves_per_sm=297, sm_throughput=97.7%) is fully SM-saturating — it is at the compute ceiling. max-autotune mode enables cuBLAS algorithm search and Triton GEMM autotuning, which can yield 5-15% improvement on large, compute-bound GEMMs by selecting the optimal tile size and pipeline stage count for the A100.

evidence.note: SM is saturated but eligible_cycles_pct is only ~59%, meaning ~41% of SM cycles are ineligible (stalled on dependencies or pipeline hazards). Autotuning can select pipeline stages that better hide these stalls.

transformation.description: Enable max-autotune mode in torch.compile to run cuBLAS algorithm search and Triton GEMM tile autotuning.
fx_steps: model = torch.compile(model, mode='max-autotune')
transformation.code_hint: max-autotune increases compilation time significantly (minutes vs. seconds for this model size). Cache the compiled artifact with TORCHINDUCTOR_CACHE_DIR if running repeatedly. Apply after OPT-1 (BF16 cast) so autotune searches over BF16 GEMM configurations.

estimated_impact.reasoning: Autotuning is most effective for large compute-bound GEMMs. The logit projection is the canonical case: [8192,512]x[512,32000] in BF16 on A100. MLPerf data shows 5-15% improvement from autotuning on similar shapes. Conservative 8% total time estimate given 99.7% GEMM workload share.

bottleneck.description: The embedding lookup is already fused with LayerNorm by Inductor into triton_red_fused_embedding_native_layer_norm_0. It accounts for only 0.13% of total attributed time with 80.7% achieved occupancy and balanced memory throughput (56.6% DRAM, 67.8% L1 active). No optimization action required or recommended for this operator.

transformation.description: No optimization needed. The Inductor Triton fusion of embedding + LayerNorm is already near-optimal for this operator. Any effort here has negligible total wall-time impact (0.13% ceiling).

estimated_impact.reasoning: Upper bound is the entire operator's time share (0.13%). Even eliminating this operator entirely would not be measurable in wall-clock benchmarks dominated by the 99.7% GEMM time.

edges.reason: Batch logit GEMMs in BF16, not FP32, to ensure the merged kernel routes to Tensor Core path
Pre-transposed weight buffers must match the BF16 dtype of runtime mm inputs
Autotuning should search over BF16 configurations; running max-autotune in FP32 wastes compile time on a suboptimal dtype

global_notes: All ncu duration values are from application-mode replay (4-8 passes); absolute timings are 2-5x longer than actual GPU execution. Use relative percentages and pct_of_peak metrics for analysis, not raw ns values.
A100 SXM4-80GB Ampere: 108 SMs, ridge point 156 FLOP/byte (BF16), 19.5 TFLOPS FP32 SIMT, 312 TFLOPS BF16 Tensor Core.
FP8 is NOT supported on Ampere (A100). FP8 optimizations require H100/Hopper or newer.

The embedding table weight (embed.weight, [32000, 512]) and logit projection weight (logits.weight, [32000, 512]) are NOT tied in this model implementation — they are separate nn.Parameter instances. BF16 cast is safe.
warp_cycles_per_instruction is null for all operators in this profile. Use eligible_cycles_pct as the latency proxy for stall analysis.

OPT-1 (BF16 cast) is the single highest-ROI transformation. The entire optimization ceiling (~99.7% of time) is addressable by this one change. Applying OPT-2 through OPT-4 without OPT-1 yields negligible gains.

If this model is used for autoregressive LM decoding (one token at a time), the batch and sequence dimensions collapse to (1, 1) — the GEMM shapes become [1, 512] x [512, 32000], which is severely wave-starved. In that regime the optimization priority order changes: speculative decoding or batching strategies become more impactful than dtype promotion.
